---
title: "Milwaukee Moved On From Giannis. Now What?"
subtitle: "The Bucks ended the greatest era in franchise history, but the reset they created is more complicated than a clean rebuild."
author: "Caleb Ramsey"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
library(tidyverse)
library(htmltools)
library(gt)

source("scripts/00_setup.R")

fig_dir <- "outputs/figures"
table_dir <- "outputs/tables"

include_table <- function(file) {
  htmltools::includeHTML(file.path(table_dir, file))
}

fig_path <- function(file) {
  file.path(fig_dir, file)
}
```

## The end of the Giannis era

Five years removed from the championship, Milwaukee has officially moved on from Giannis Antetokounmpo.

It is easy to remember how it ended. The last few seasons got messy. The roster got older. The margin for error kept shrinking. The Damian Lillard swing never stabilized the way Milwaukee needed it to. Khris Middleton was eventually moved for Kyle Kuzma, a pivot that felt less like a title push and more like the first public sign that the old version of the Bucks was gone. Then the final injury blow came, the window closed, and Milwaukee was forced into the kind of reset it had avoided for more than a decade.

But the ending should not flatten what came before it.

Giannis arrived as a teenage mystery and became the best player in franchise history. He gave Milwaukee an identity, a title, multiple MVP-level seasons, and a decade of relevance that most small-market teams never get. The Bucks did not just have a good run. They had an era.

```{r giannis-pra}
knitr::include_graphics(fig_path("giannis_production_trend.png"))
```

The production arc tells the basic story. Giannis did not gradually become a useful star. He became the nightly engine for a franchise that eventually built everything around his pressure, his downhill force, his rebounding, and his defensive range.

```{r giannis-resume}
include_table("giannis_accomplishments_table.html")
```

This is why the trade is not just a transaction. It is a franchise handoff. Milwaukee is not replacing one player. It is replacing the player who made the last decade possible.

## How high the Bucks climbed

The regular-season transformation was dramatic. Milwaukee went from a team trying to build around promise to a yearly contender. At the peak, the Bucks were not just good. They were a stable regular-season machine with a championship ceiling.

```{r win-pct}
knitr::include_graphics(fig_path("bucks_win_pct_trend.png"))
```

The regular-season baseline lasted longer than the playoff ceiling. That is what made the end feel so strange. Milwaukee remained recognizable as a serious team for a while, but the postseason evidence kept getting worse. After the 2021 title, the Bucks never got back past Round 2.

```{r playoff-table}
include_table("playoff_results_article_table.html")
```

Injuries explain part of that. Middleton’s 2022 injury changed the repeat attempt. Giannis’ 2024 injury wrecked the first Dame postseason. Dame’s Achilles ended the last version of the title chase. But the larger story is that the Bucks had become increasingly fragile. They needed too many things to go right.

```{r transaction-timeline}
knitr::include_graphics(fig_path("transaction_timeline.png"))
```

The Middleton-for-Kuzma pivot is important because it was the moment the old title core stopped looking like a title core. Milwaukee was still trying to reshape the roster around Giannis, but the moves no longer felt like clean upgrades. They felt like compromises.

## What Milwaukee got from Miami

The return from Miami is not one obvious successor. It is a portfolio of bets.

Tyler Herro gives Milwaukee a real scorer and offensive stabilizer, but he may be more bridge piece than long-term centerpiece. Jaime Jaquez Jr. is the safest basketball player in the return, a physical connector wing with feel and finishing touch, but the jumper decides how scalable he becomes. Kel’el Ware is the upside swing. He has the tools to become a high-impact rim protector, vertical spacer, and real shooting threat at center, but the passing, self-creation, consistency, and motor questions matter. Kasparas Jakucionis is the developmental guard bet, with shooting, passing, and pick-and-roll flashes, but athletic limitations could cap the ceiling.

```{r miami-profile}
include_table("miami_return_profile_table.html")
```

The cleanest skill signal in the return is shooting. Herro is the known volume spacer. Kasparas has the loudest pure shooting indicator in the group. Ware’s profile is different: for a young big, his three-point rate and accuracy are real spacing signals. Jaquez is the swing question.

```{r miami-shooting}
include_table("miami_shooting_table.html")
```

The playtype data helps separate what these players actually do from the general “young talent” label. Herro’s value is more balanced across creation, spacing, transition, and finishing. Jaquez’s profile is more uneven: the finishing pops, but the creation and spacing efficiency are the concerns.

```{r herro-jaquez-playtypes}
knitr::include_graphics(fig_path("miami_return_playtype_profile_herro_jaquez.png"))
```

Ware and Kasparas are different kinds of development bets. Ware’s ceiling is tied to whether the rim protection, vertical play, and shooting become stable enough to offset the skill gaps. Kasparas is more of a connector and playmaking bet, with real three-point upside and pick-and-roll feel, but less athletic margin.

```{r ware-kasparas-playtypes}
knitr::include_graphics(fig_path("miami_return_playtype_profile_ware_kasparas.png"))
```

## The lottery swings

The Miami return gives Milwaukee actual young pieces to evaluate, but the reset only becomes interesting if the draft part hits too.

Brayden Burries is the cleaner translation bet. He was the best player on a dominant Arizona team, showed a versatile scoring arsenal, made an impact in transition, and has enough two-way utility to fit next to Ryan Rollins. The self-creation is not perfect, but the role is easier to imagine.

Ament is the more volatile swing. The tools are obvious: size, movement, shot-making flashes, defensive versatility, and the kind of forward frame teams chase. The concern is that so much of the evaluation is still built on projection. His efficiency, finishing, feel, consistency, and processing all leave real questions. That does not kill the upside, but it changes how cleanly you can bet on it.

```{r draft-prospects}
include_table("draft_prospects_table.html")
```

Burries is the safer bet to become useful. Ament is the one who can change the ceiling if the flashes become structure.

## The pick situation is still bleak

The reset added Miami upside, but it did not give Milwaukee the kind of clean pick control most rebuilding teams want.

```{r future-picks}
include_table("future_pick_article_table.html")
```

That is the tension. Milwaukee has more young talent than it did before the trade, but it still does not control the board cleanly right away. The Bucks can get better from here, but they cannot just bottom out with total freedom. The Miami picks matter. The internal development matters more.

## What the reset can become

The reset is not built around one obvious franchise player. It is built around multiple different bets.

Burries is the cleanest basketball fit. Ament is the high-variance forward swing. Ware might have the highest ceiling if the tools translate into reliable habits. Kasparas is the connector guard with shooting and pick-and-roll upside. Rollins matters too, because he gives Milwaukee another two-way guard with current utility and offensive upside.

The problem is that each path has a different failure point.

```{r reset-paths}
include_table("reset_paths_table.html")
```

The best version of this reset is real. Ware becomes a two-way frontcourt anchor. Ament’s flashes become structure. Burries becomes a high-level two-way starter. Kasparas becomes a rotation connector with enough shooting and playmaking to matter. That is not impossible.

But the bad version is not hard to see either. Ament never turns projection into production. Ware’s motor and processing concerns harden. Burries is useful but lacks enough self-creation to scale. Kasparas cannot separate enough athletically. In that version, Milwaukee has young talent, but no clear engine.

## Where that leaves Milwaukee

Milwaukee did the thing that eventually had to happen. The Giannis era had run its course, and the final version of the roster no longer looked close enough to justify pretending otherwise.

That does not make the trade painless. It does not make the reset clean. It does not guarantee that the next core is already here.

What it does give Milwaukee is a start.

The Bucks now have a younger timeline, multiple developmental swings, some Miami pick upside, and enough useful pieces to avoid starting from zero. That is not the same as having a future star. It is not the same as having a clean rebuild. It is not the same as replacing Giannis.

It is simply the next version of the franchise trying to find itself after the greatest player it ever had.

## Appendix

### DataBallr snapshot

```{r databallr-snapshot}
include_table("miami_return_metrics_table.html")
```

### Full playtype table

```{r databallr-playtypes}
include_table("databallr_playtype_table.html")
```

### Detailed future pick control

```{r appendix-picks}
include_table("appendix_future_pick_control_table.html")
```

### Year-by-year team results

```{r appendix-team-results}
include_table("appendix_team_results_table.html")
```

### Transaction summary

```{r appendix-transactions}
include_table("appendix_transaction_summary_table.html")
```

## Methodology notes

This report uses manually collected and cleaned data from Basketball Reference, ESPN, RealGM, NBA.com-style transaction research, DataBallr screenshots, and manually entered draft/prospect references.

DataBallr metrics were manually entered from player dashboard screenshots. Percentile ranks are based on positional-ranked percentiles, so they are used as contextual benchmarks rather than raw leaguewide ranks.

Playtype buckets follow DataBallr’s framework: Creation includes handler, isolation, and post-up usage; Spacing includes spot-up, off-screen, and handoff actions; Transition captures open-floor scoring; Finishing includes cuts, rolls, putbacks, and similar interior finish playtypes.

The Ament discussion is informed by broader scouting context, especially the projection-versus-production tension: tools, size, movement, and skill flashes on one side; efficiency, finishing, feel, and consistency concerns on the other.
```
